params.label: Background
params.notes: This first section sets up WHY the project matters before any code: what head-CT AI actually does in a hospital, and why any fairness question needs data about who each scan came from. Keep it concrete -- the payoff is the missing-metadata twist later.

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.60
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g3_brain_ct/slides/figures/intro_triage_queue.png', left=body_l, top=body_top + 0.15, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h - 0.30)
px = body_l + fw + 0.35
pw = body_w - fw - 0.35
_add_card(slide, surface_rgb=_rgb('#FFFFFF'), label='Triage, not diagnosis',
          body='In the ER a bleed is an emergency and minutes matter. Deployed head-CT AI -- Chilamkurthy 2018 on ~313,000 scans, the RSNA 2019 challenge, Seyam 2022 -- reorders the radiologist reading queue so likely bleeds are read first. Real tools clear ~0.90+ AUC, and still do worse on some bleed subtypes.',
          left=px, top=body_top + 0.20, width=pw, height=body_h - 0.55, accent_rgb=accent_rgb)
params.notes:
Open concrete. The single most real, deployed use of AI on brain CT is triage: a network scans the whole
stack and pushes probable bleeds to the top of the list so a radiologist reads the dangerous ones first,
instead of in arrival order. In a busy ER that saves minutes that matter for a stroke. Ground it in real
systems: Chilamkurthy 2018 (Lancet) trained on ~313,000 head CTs at AUC>0.90; the RSNA 2019 challenge had
60+ neuroradiologists label 25,000+ exams; Seyam 2022 is a deployed tool at ~93% accuracy. Then the catch:
these tools are NOT equally good on every case. Transition: to even ask 'fair for whom', the data has to
record who each scan came from.
params.title: Head-CT triage AI, in one picture

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.60
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g3_brain_ct/slides/figures/intro_datasheet_checklist.png', left=body_l, top=body_top + 0.15, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h - 0.30)
px = body_l + fw + 0.35
pw = body_w - fw - 0.35
_add_card(slide, surface_rgb=_rgb('#FFFFFF'), label='Who is in the data?',
          body='To test fairness you compare accuracy across groups -- age, sex, race, scanner. That only works if the dataset records who each scan came from. Datasheets for Datasets (Gebru 2021) says every dataset should ship that documentation; radiology-AI reviews (Tripathi 2023) show it often does not. Ours has only the image and a label.',
          left=px, top=body_top + 0.20, width=pw, height=body_h - 0.55, accent_rgb=accent_rgb)
params.notes:
This is the pivot from 'cool tool' to 'the real lesson'. A fairness check is a comparison ACROSS groups, so
it needs the grouping columns to exist. The Datasheets for Datasets idea (Gebru 2021) proposes every dataset
ship a standard sheet answering who is in it, how it was labeled, and its limits. Reviews (Tripathi 2023)
find radiology datasets routinely leave those blank. Left figure: the six things a datasheet asks vs. the two
our brain-CT set actually has -- four demographic rows are empty. Transition: and empty rows mean unfillable
audit bars.
params.title: A dataset should tell you who is in it

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.60
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g3_brain_ct/slides/figures/intro_unauditable_bias.png', left=body_l, top=body_top + 0.15, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h - 0.30)
px = body_l + fw + 0.35
pw = body_w - fw - 0.35
_add_card(slide, surface_rgb=_rgb('#FFFFFF'), label='Blank bars',
          body='Real audits found imaging AI that missed disease more often in female, Black, and low-income patients (Seyyed-Kalantari 2021), and models that secretly lean on demographic shortcuts (Yang 2024). You can only SEE those failures if the data records the groups. When it does not, every per-group accuracy bar is a blank you cannot fill.',
          left=px, top=body_top + 0.20, width=pw, height=body_h - 0.55, accent_rgb=accent_rgb)
params.notes:
Make the abstract concrete with real findings. Seyyed-Kalantari 2021 (Nature Medicine): chest-X-ray AI gave
more false 'no finding' calls for female, Black, and low-income patients -- underdiagnosis that only showed
up BECAUSE those datasets recorded the groups. Yang 2024 (Nature Medicine): imaging AI often rides demographic
shortcuts, so fairness measured on one hospital does not transfer. The figure equates missing metadata with a
blind spot: one measurable bar, the rest drawn as empty dashed outlines labeled 'no data -- cannot measure'.
Transition: now our own build, on exactly such a dataset.
params.title: You can’t audit a bias you can’t see

params.label: Methods
params.notes: Now we pin down exactly what we built: the classification task, the bare dataset we were handed, and the one reusable idea -- transfer learning -- that makes it work on so few scans. A short setup section before the results.

params.code:
gap = 0.35
cw = (body_w - 2 * gap) / 3
h = body_h * 0.50
y = body_top + 0.12
_add_card(slide, surface_rgb=_rgb('#FFFFFF'), label='Task', body='Classify one 64x64 brain CT slice as normal or stroke -- a clean, well-posed binary question, exactly the kind transfer learning handles well.', left=body_l, top=y, width=cw, height=h, accent_rgb=TURQUOISE_RGB)
_add_card(slide, surface_rgb=_rgb('#FFFFFF'), label='Given', body='The CT image, and a normal / stroke label. That is the entire dataset -- one picture in, one word out.', left=body_l + cw + gap, top=y, width=cw, height=h, accent_rgb=AMBER_RGB)
_add_card(slide, surface_rgb=_rgb('#FFFFFF'), label='Missing', body='Age, sex, race, scanner, site, time-since-injury -- every field a fairness audit would need. Not one of them was recorded.', left=body_l + 2 * (cw + gap), top=y, width=cw, height=h, accent_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB)
ry = y + h + body_h * 0.10
_add_rect(slide, left=body_l, top=ry, width=0.07, height=body_h * 0.20, fill_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB)
_add_text(slide, 'Hold onto that empty "Missing" column -- it turns out to be the whole finding of this project.', left=body_l + 0.30, top=ry, width=body_w - 0.30, height=body_h * 0.20, size=18, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT, italic=True, anchor=MSO_ANCHOR.MIDDLE)
params.notes:
State the problem and, deliberately, the absence. The task is a clean binary: normal vs. stroke on a 64x64
brain slice. What the model is GIVEN is only the image and the label. What is MISSING is every field a
fairness audit needs -- age, sex, race, scanner, site, time-since-injury. The pink 'Missing' card is the one
to linger on; it is the whole story of this project set up in advance. Transition: how we actually build the
detector.
params.title: The task and the data

params.code:
_add_text(slide, 'The same trick that won Day 1. Rather than teach a network to see from zero on a few hundred scans, we take a ResNet18 already trained on a million everyday photos, freeze what it learned about edges and texture, and train only a small new head to map those features to normal vs. stroke. Borrowed knowledge beats from-scratch when data is small.', left=body_l, top=body_top + 0.10, width=body_w, height=1.25, size=16, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
steps = ['Pretrained ResNet18', 'Freeze the body', 'Train a new 2-class head']
subs = ['Learned edges, shapes, and texture from ~1M everyday photos.', 'Keep those features frozen -- do not spend our few scans re-learning them.', 'Train only this small layer to map features to normal vs. stroke.']
cols = [TURQUOISE_RGB, AMBER_RGB, DEEPPINK_RGB]
gap = 0.55
cw = (body_w - 2 * gap) / 3
y = body_top + 1.70
ch = body_h * 0.30
for i in range(3):
    x = body_l + i * (cw + gap)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=y, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=SURFACE_RGB)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=y, width=cw, height=0.10, fill_rgb=cols[i])
    _add_text(slide, steps[i], left=x + 0.22, top=y + 0.26, width=cw - 0.44, height=0.5, size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    _add_text(slide, subs[i], left=x + 0.22, top=y + 0.80, width=cw - 0.44, height=ch - 0.90, size=12, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
    if i < 2:
        _add_text(slide, '>', left=x + cw + 0.13, top=y + ch * 0.30, width=0.35, height=0.5, size=24, color_rgb=cols[i], font=MONO_FONT, bold=True, anchor=MSO_ANCHOR.MIDDLE)
ty = y + ch + body_h * 0.08
_add_rect(slide, left=body_l, top=ty, width=0.07, height=body_h * 0.17, fill_rgb=TURQUOISE_RGB)
_add_text(slide, 'The habit: when data is small, start from something already pretrained -- never train a big network from scratch.', left=body_l + 0.30, top=ty, width=body_w - 0.30, height=body_h * 0.17, size=17, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT, anchor=MSO_ANCHOR.MIDDLE)
params.notes:
One transferable idea, echoed from Day 1: transfer learning. On a few hundred scans you cannot teach a network
to see from scratch, so you borrow a ResNet18 already trained on a million ImageNet photos, freeze the body
that learned generic edges and textures, and train only a small new 2-class head. The chip flow makes the
three moves concrete: pretrained backbone, freeze, new head. Emphasize the habit: when data is small, start
from something pretrained. Transition: does it work? Grade it honestly.
params.title: The idea: reuse a pretrained network

params.label: Results
params.notes: Three honest checks on the model we trained: does it catch strokes, is it looking at the right pixels, and does it survive a noisier scan. The theme is honest evaluation -- we report the weaknesses, not just the headline accuracy.

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.50
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g3_brain_ct/slides/figures/confusion.png', left=body_l, top=body_top + 0.15, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h - 0.30)
px = body_l + fw + 0.40
pw = body_w - fw - 0.40
_add_text(slide, '0.84', left=px, top=body_top + 0.15, width=pw, height=1.15,
          size=66, color_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, 'sensitivity -- of the real strokes, the share we CAUGHT', left=px, top=body_top + 1.35,
          width=pw, height=0.7, size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
_add_text(slide, 'Accuracy is only ~0.71 and specificity ~0.60, so it false-alarms on many normal scans. For a safety-net triage tool, catching bleeds matters more than the false alarms -- and one accuracy number alone would have hidden that split.',
          left=px, top=body_top + 2.15, width=pw, height=body_h - 2.35, size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
params.notes:
The honest-evaluation slide, and the metric lesson. On the held-out test set overall accuracy is ~0.71, but
that is the wrong headline for screening. Break it into the two error types: sensitivity ~0.84 means it
catches most real strokes (the error we most want to avoid), while specificity ~0.60 means it false-alarms on
many normal scans. The confusion matrix shows the same split as a picture. Point: for a triage safety-net you
would tune toward sensitivity on purpose -- crying wolf beats missing a bleed -- and a single accuracy number
would have hidden it. Transition: but is it right for the right reason?
params.title: It catches most strokes — but cries wolf

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.60
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g3_brain_ct/slides/figures/gradcam.png', left=body_l, top=body_top + 0.15, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h - 0.30)
px = body_l + fw + 0.35
pw = body_w - fw - 0.35
_add_card(slide, surface_rgb=_rgb('#FFFFFF'), label='Right for the wrong reason?',
          body='Grad-CAM paints WHERE the network looked. Some heat lands on brain tissue, but some drifts to the skull edge and the image border -- a classic shortcut that works on this dataset and would break on a new scanner (Yang 2024). Worth saying out loud, not hiding.',
          left=px, top=body_top + 0.20, width=pw, height=body_h - 0.55, accent_rgb=accent_rgb)
params.notes:
Transparency audit. A model can be right for the wrong reason, so Grad-CAM overlays where the network actually
looked to make each call. Honest read of our figure: some heat is on brain tissue, but some sits on the skull
edge and the black border -- a shortcut. Tie it to Yang 2024: shortcut-learning is exactly why 'fair here'
does not generalize. The teaching move is to SAY this out loud; naming a weakness is a stronger talk than
hiding it. Transition: and it is brittle when the image gets messy.
params.title: Is it reading the brain, or cheating off the skull?

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.58
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g3_brain_ct/slides/figures/noise_robustness.png', left=body_l, top=body_top + 0.15, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h - 0.30)
px = body_l + fw + 0.35
pw = body_w - fw - 0.35
_add_card(slide, surface_rgb=_rgb('#FFFFFF'), label='Brittle under noise',
          body='Training images are clean; a real ER scanner is noisier -- motion, low dose, different machines. Adding random noise to the test scans makes accuracy sag fast. A deployable tool would need augmentation, far more data, and live monitoring after it ships.',
          left=px, top=body_top + 0.20, width=pw, height=body_h - 0.55, accent_rgb=accent_rgb)
params.notes:
Robustness / monitoring audit. Training data is clean; deployed scanners are messy (motion, low dose, other
machines). We simulate that by adding increasing noise to the test images and watching accuracy -- it drops
quickly, so this toy model is brittle. The real-world lesson: a shipped model meets drift, so it needs
augmentation, more data, and live monitoring, not a one-time score. Transition: now the audit we cannot run
at all -- the point of the whole project.
params.title: Does it survive a messier scan?

params.label: The gap that IS the finding
params.notes: This is the turn. Everything so far was a normal ML project; now we name the thing this dataset makes impossible -- a fairness audit -- and argue that the missing metadata is not a footnote but the headline finding.

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.58
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g3_brain_ct/slides/figures/missing_metadata.png', left=body_l, top=body_top + 0.15, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h - 0.30)
px = body_l + fw + 0.35
pw = body_w - fw - 0.35
_add_card(slide, surface_rgb=_rgb('#FFFFFF'), label='Nothing to group by',
          body='We CAN audit fairness by class, because the label exists. But the audit that matters -- does it work as well for older patients as younger, women as men, one scanner as another -- cannot run at all. The dataset never recorded age, sex, race, or scanner. There is literally nothing to group by.',
          left=px, top=body_top + 0.20, width=pw, height=body_h - 0.55, accent_rgb=accent_rgb)
params.notes:
The climax figure: a deliberately near-blank slide, a picture of metadata that does not exist. Draw the
distinction crisply: we CAN audit by class (normal vs. stroke) because the label is recorded, but the audit
that actually matters for fairness -- across age, sex, race, scanner -- cannot be run at all, because there is
nothing to group by. Not hard; impossible. Let the emptiness of the figure do the work. Transition: why that
silence is disqualifying, not a caveat.
params.section_label: The gap that IS the finding
params.title: The audit we literally cannot run

params.code:
_add_text(slide, 'An un-runnable fairness audit is not a footnote.', left=body_l, top=body_top + body_h * 0.20, width=body_w, height=1.1, size=30, color_rgb=accent_rgb, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True, align=PP_ALIGN.CENTER)
_add_rect(slide, left=body_l + body_w * 0.42, top=body_top + body_h * 0.42, width=body_w * 0.16, height=0.035, fill_rgb=accent_rgb)
_add_text(slide, 'Our detector might be much worse for some group of patients, and we would have no way to know. Datasheets work and radiology-AI reviews say a dataset should document who is in it; real audits show hidden bias appears exactly along age, sex, race, and scanner lines -- and only where those labels exist. For a tool that would touch patients, that silence is the headline.', left=body_l + 1.0, top=body_top + body_h * 0.52, width=body_w - 2.0, height=body_h * 0.40, size=16, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT, align=PP_ALIGN.CENTER)
params.notes:
Land the thesis. Say it plainly: the model could be far worse for some group and we could not detect it. The
literature agrees on both halves -- datasheets/reviews (Gebru 2021, Tripathi 2023) say document who is in the
data; audits (Seyyed-Kalantari 2021, Yang 2024) show bias hides along demographic and scanner lines and is
only visible where those labels exist. So for anything that would touch a patient, an un-runnable fairness
audit is not a caveat you note at the end -- it is the finding. This is the sentence to end the talk on.
Transition: the sources behind all of this.
params.section_label: The gap that IS the finding
params.title: Why that silence is disqualifying

params.label: References
params.notes: The sources behind every claim, grouped by the role each plays: the real triage systems that motivate the task, the dataset-documentation argument, and the fairness audits that only work when metadata exists.

params.code:
gap = 0.35
cw = (body_w - 2 * gap) / 3
h = body_h * 0.52
y = body_top + 0.22
_add_card(slide, surface_rgb=_rgb('#FFFFFF'), label='Head-CT triage AI', body='Chilamkurthy 2018 (The Lancet); Flanders 2020 (Radiology: AI, RSNA challenge); Seyam 2022 (Radiology: AI).', left=body_l, top=y, width=cw, height=h, accent_rgb=TURQUOISE_RGB)
_add_card(slide, surface_rgb=_rgb('#FFFFFF'), label='Datasets & documentation', body='Gebru 2021 (Communications of the ACM, Datasheets for Datasets); Tripathi 2023 (J Am Coll Radiol).', left=body_l + cw + gap, top=y, width=cw, height=h, accent_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB)
_add_card(slide, surface_rgb=_rgb('#FFFFFF'), label='Fairness audits that need metadata', body='Seyyed-Kalantari 2021 (Nature Medicine); Yang 2024 (Nature Medicine).', left=body_l + 2 * (cw + gap), top=y, width=cw, height=h, accent_rgb=AMBER_RGB)
ry = y + h + body_h * 0.09
_add_rect(slide, left=body_l, top=ry, width=0.07, height=body_h * 0.16, fill_rgb=TURQUOISE_RGB)
_add_text(slide, 'Every claim in this deck traces back to one of these sources.', left=body_l + 0.30, top=ry, width=body_w - 0.30, height=body_h * 0.16, size=17, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT, anchor=MSO_ANCHOR.MIDDLE)
params.notes:
The evidence base, grouped by role. Left: the real head-CT triage AI that motivates the task (Chilamkurthy
2018, Flanders/RSNA 2020, Seyam 2022). Middle: the datasheets/documentation argument (Gebru 2021, Tripathi
2023). Right: the fairness audits that only work when metadata exists (Seyyed-Kalantari 2021, Yang 2024).
Every claim in the deck traces to one of these.
params.title: References
